_meta.vertical: Residential wildlife removal (raccoons, squirrels, bats, skunks, opossums, birds)
_meta.source_note: Aggregated from large outbound call-data studies (millions of calls) plus residential home-services nuances. Validate per-market with your own dial logs.

core_windows.label: Late morning sweet spot
core_windows.note: 9–10 a.m. is the single strongest hour in most studies; 10–11 a.m. is the working-but-not-distracted window.

core_windows.label: Late afternoon wrap-up
core_windows.note: Consistently tests as near-highest engagement; prospects are wrapping the day, not yet in evening routine.

experimental_blocks.label: Working-homeowner evening
experimental_blocks.note: Productive if script is tight and respectful of family time.

experimental_blocks.label: Saturday morning
experimental_blocks.note: Under-used for residential home services. Verify any local TCPA / time-of-day rules before dialing.

avoid_windows.label: Lunchtime dip
avoid_windows.reason: Answer + engagement rates drop during pure lunch hours.

avoid_days.reason: Lowest quality for cold calls in most studies.

homeowner_nuances: Wildlife removal is emergency-driven — answer rate spikes in the 24-48 hours after a sighting or noise complaint. If you have inbound web traffic data, prioritize callbacks within 60 minutes.
Most calls go to personal cells, not landlines. Expect mixed workday + off-hours behavior.
Hot lead window: 5-7 p.m. for working homeowners who couldn't deal with the wildlife issue during the day.
Avoid scheduled dial blasts during local school pickup hours (2:30-3:30 p.m.) — answer rate drops sharply.
